---
title: "Markdown and Quarto features I use"
description: "A quick tour of footnotes, math, code blocks, and callouts."
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-15"
categories: [quarto, reference]
---

This post is a small reference for the markdown and Quarto features
that show up most often in academic writing. Keep it around as a
cheat sheet, or delete it once you've seen how each piece works.

## Footnotes

You can drop a footnote anywhere in your prose.[^1] Quarto auto-numbers
them and adds a link back to where you cited them — handy for academic
writing.

[^1]: Like this one. Footnote definitions can live anywhere in the file;
they're collected at the bottom of the rendered page.

## Math

Quarto understands **LaTeX-style math**, so you can write an equation
inline like $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, or as a display
block on its own line:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

No setup required — Quarto renders it via MathJax automatically.

## Code blocks

You can show code in a fenced block. Note the lowercase `r` with
**no curly braces** — that means "display this as code, don't run it":

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "A plot I am not actually running")
```

(Swap ` ```r ` for ` ```{r} ` and Quarto would *execute* the chunk —
but that's a topic for another day.)

## Callouts

::: {.callout-tip}
## Callouts are a Quarto feature
This coloured box is a **callout**. There are five types: `note`, `tip`,
`warning`, `important`, and `caution`. They're great for highlighting
things without breaking the flow of your writing.
:::

## More

For everything else, the [Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/)
is the canonical reference.
